question: What is the range of HbA1c values that indicates prediabetes according to the text?
source_text: Meta-analyses of 25 RCTs (n=14,230) demonstrate that metformin reduces HbA1c by 1.0-1.5% from baseline, with a favourable safety profile. The UKPDS 34 trial demonstrated a 39% reduction in myocardial infarction and 36% reduction in all-cause mortality in overweight T2DM patients treated with metformin vs conventional therapy. Common adverse effects include gastrointestinal symptoms (nausea, diarrhoea), which are minimised by titrating the dose slowly and taking with food. Diagnostic Test | Diagn

question: What is the relationship between the number of parameters in the inference-time models and the cross-entropy model using the same encoder?
ground_truth: The inference-time models contain exactly the same number of parameters as a cross-entropy model using the same encoder.
source_text: We again normalize the output of this network to lie on the unit hypersphere, which enables using an inner product to measure distances in the projection space. As in self-supervised contrastive learning [48, 3], we discard Proj(·) at the end of contrastive training. As a result, our inference-time models contain exactly the same number of parameters as a cross-entropy model using the same encoder, Enc(·). 3.2 Contrastive Loss Functions Given this framework, we now look at the family of contrast

question: What does the self-supervised contrastive loss use as positives in its approach?
ground_truth: The self-supervised contrastive loss uses an augmented version of the same image as positives.
source_text: Figure 2: Supervised vs. self-supervised contrastive losses: The self-supervised contrastive loss (left, Eq. 1) contrasts a single positive for each anchor (i.e., an augmented version of the same image) against a set of negatives consisting of the entire remainder of the batch. The supervised contrastive loss (right) considered in this paper (Eq. 2), however, contrasts the set of all samples from the same class as positives against the negatives from the remainder of the batch. As demonstrated b

question: What is the fasting plasma glucose (FPG) threshold for diagnosing Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (T2DM) according to the WHO and ADA 2023 guidelines?
ground_truth: >= 126 mg/dL (7.0 mmol/L)
source_text: representing 9% of global health expenditure. The pathophysiology of T2DM involves a complex interplay of genetic susceptibility, environmental factors, and lifestyle behaviours. Key mechanisms include peripheral insulin resistance in skeletal muscle and adipose tissue, impaired hepatic glucose production suppression, and progressive decline in pancreatic beta-cell mass and function. Central obesity, physical inactivity, and unhealthy dietary patterns are major modifiable risk factors. Clinical

question: Which optimizer combination yielded the best performance for SupCon on ImageNet?
ground_truth: LARS for pre-training and RMSProp for training the linear layer.
source_text: We experimented with standard optimizers such as LARS [59], RMSProp [20] and SGD with momentum [39] in different permutations for the initial pre-training step and train- ing of the dense layer. While SGD with momentum works best for training ResNets with cross-entropy, we get the best performance for SupCon on ImageNet by using LARS for pre-training and RMSProp to training the linear layer. For CIFAR10 and CIFAR100 SGD with momentum performed best. Additional results for combinations of optimiz

question: What is the performance comparison of SupCon loss and cross-entropy in terms of top-1 accuracy across various augmentations and optimizers?
ground_truth: SupCon loss shows significantly lower variance in output compared to cross-entropy, and when batch size is varied while holding other hyperparameters constant, SupCon consistently achieves better top-1 accuracy.
source_text: In Fig. 4(a), we compare the top-1 accuracy of SupCon loss against cross-entropy across changes in augmentations (RandAugment [6], AutoAugment [5], SimAugment [3], Stacked RandAugment [49]); optimizers (LARS, SGD with Momentum and RMSProp); and learning rates. We observe significantly lower variance in the output of the contrastive loss. Note that batch sizes for cross-entropy and supervised contrastive are the same, thus ruling out any batch-size effects. question: What are the two most straightforward ways to generalize the contrastive loss for supervised learning as presented in the text?
ground_truth: The two most straightforward ways to generalize the contrastive loss for supervised learning are Lsup out and Lsup in.

question: What are some important practical differences between the method discussed in the text and the Compact Clustering via Label Propagation (CCLP) regularizer?
ground_truth: The important practical differences include normalization of the contrastive embedding onto the unit sphere, tuning of a temperature parameter in the contrastive objective, and stronger augmentation.
source_text: Most similar to our method is the Compact Clustering via Label Propagation (CCLP) regularizer in Kamnitsas et. al. [24]. While CCLP focuses mostly on the semi-supervised case, in the fully supervised case the regularizer reduces to almost exactly our loss formulation. Important practical differences include our normalization of the contrastive embedding onto the unit sphere, tuning of a temperature parameter in the contrastive objective, and stronger augmentation. Additionally, Kamnitsas et. al.

question: What was added in Version 2 of the change log regarding supervised contrastive loss?
ground_truth: Analysis of different forms of supervised contrastive loss and its gradients as well as experimental results to back up the analysis.
source_text: 15 Change Log Version 1 (2020-04-23) Initial Arxiv version. Version 2 (2020-10-22) Added analysis of different forms of supervised contrastive loss and its gradients as well as experimental results to back up the analysis. Additional tuning for better top-1 accuracy. Added transfer learning experiments. Moved accuracy vs num positives to supplemen- tary. More heavily tuned models resulted in deterioration of ECE. question: How does the number of positives and negatives affect the gradient contribution in Lsup out and Lsup in?
ground_truth: Increasing the number of negatives can make the gradient response to a hard positive larger in any individual training step, and for Lsup out, increasing the number of positives can also make the gradient response larger.

question: What is the relationship between the gradient of the supervised loss with respect to wi and zi?
ground_truth: The gradient of the supervised loss with respect to wi is related to that with respect to zi via the chain rule.
source_text: positive/negative mining, it will be shown to be true for all three contrastive losses (self-supervised and both supervised versions). question: What is the target blood pressure for most T2DM patients?
ground_truth: Target < 130/80 mmHg in most T2DM patients.
source_text: Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) is increasingly recommended in T2DM patients on insulin or at high risk of hypoglycaemia. CGM-derived metrics including Time in Range (TIR: glucose 70-180 mg/dL) correlate with HbA1c and predict complications. A TIR > 70% corresponds approximately to HbA1c < 7%. 6. question: Which augmentation strategy provided the highest Top-1 accuracy on ResNet-50?
ground_truth: AutoAugment
source_text: The strategy involves an additional RandAugment step before doing the color jitter as done in SimAugment. This leads to a more diverse set of images created by the augmentation and hence more robust training which generalizes better. and found that AutoAugment [30] gave us the highest Top-1 accuracy on ResNet-50 for both the cross entropy loss and supervised contrastive loss.
